   375→- **INSERT:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/insert
   376→
   377→## Schema Definition
   378→
   379→- **DEFINE TABLE:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/table
   380→- **DEFINE FIELD (types, defaults, assertions):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/field
   381→- **DEFINE INDEX (unique, full-text, vector):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/indexes
   382→- **DEFINE EVENT (triggers on record changes):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/event
   383→- **DEFINE FUNCTION (reusable SurrealQL functions):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/function
   384→
   385→## Schema Migrations
   386→
   387→- **CLI Reference:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli
   388→- **Validate migration files (`surreal validate`):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli/validate
   389→- **Apply migrations (`surreal import`):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli/import
   390→- **Export backup snapshots (`surreal export`):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli/export
   391→- **ALTER statement:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/alter
   392→- **REMOVE statement:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/remove
   393→- **BEGIN / COMMIT transaction:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/begin
   394→- **INFO statement (post-migration verification):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/info
   395→
   396→## Data Migrations
   397→
   398→- **Export backups (`surreal export`):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli/export
   399→- **Apply migration scripts (`surreal import`):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli/import
   400→- **UPDATE statement (bulk row transforms):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/update
   401→- **FOR statement (iterative transforms):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/for
   402→- **RELATE statement (edge rewrites):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/relate
   403→- **REMOVE statement (drop old fields):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/remove
   404→- **BEGIN / COMMIT transaction:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/begin
   405→
   406→## Graph Relationships (Critical for our data model)
   407→
   408→- **Graph Model Overview (best practices, when to use edges vs record links):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/models/graph
   409→- **RELATE Statement (create edges):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/relate
   410→- **Graph Relations Fundamentals (RELATE, INSERT RELATION, arrow syntax):** https://surrealdb.com/learn/fundamentals/relationships/graph-relations
   411→- **Three Ways to Model Relationships (record links vs references vs graph edges):** https://surrealdb.com/blog/three-ways-to-model-data-relationships-in-surrealdb
   412→- **Graph Traversal, Recursion & Shortest Path:** https://surrealdb.com/blog/data-analysis-using-graph-traversal-recursion-and-shortest-path
   413→
   414→### Key SurrealQL graph syntax:
   415→```sql
   416→-- Create edge
   417→RELATE person:marcus->owns->project:brain;
   418→
   419→-- Traverse forward
   420→SELECT ->owns->project FROM person:marcus;
   421→
   422→-- Traverse backward
   423→SELECT <-owns<-person FROM project:brain;
   424→
   425→-- Multi-hop
   426→SELECT ->has_feature->feature->has_task->task FROM project:brain;
   427→
   428→-- Bidirectional
   429→SELECT <->conflicts_with<->decision FROM decision:d1;
   430→
   431→-- Edge with metadata
   432→RELATE decision:d1->conflicts_with->decision:d2
   433→  SET severity = 'hard', description = 'Contradictory deadlines';
   434→
   435→-- Recursive traversal (org tree, dependency chains)
   436→record:root.{..}.{ id, ->depends_on->task.@ };
   437→
   438→-- TYPE RELATION enforces edge-only tables
   439→DEFINE TABLE owns TYPE RELATION IN person OUT project | feature | task;
   440→```
   441→
   442→## Vector Search (For semantic search + RAG context)
   443→
   444→- **Vector Search Reference Guide:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/reference-guide/vector-search
   445→- **DEFINE INDEX (HNSW & MTREE):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/indexes
   446→- **OpenAI Embeddings Integration:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/integrations/embeddings/openai
   447→- **Mistral Embeddings Integration:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/integrations/embeddings/mistral
   448→- **Python Embeddings (patterns transferable to JS):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/integrations/embeddings/python
   449→- **Full-Text to Vector Search Migration Guide:** https://surrealdb.com/blog/moving-from-full-text-search-to-vector-search-in-surrealdb
   450→- **Hybrid Search (vector + full-text with RRF):** https://surrealdb.com/blog/hybrid-vector-text-search-in-the-terminal-with-surrealdb-and-ratatui
   451→- **Search Functions (search::score, search::rrf):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/functions/database/search
   452→- **Vector Functions (distance, similarity):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/functions/database/vector
   453→
   454→### Key SurrealQL vector syntax:
   455→```sql
   456→-- Define embedding field + HNSW index
   457→DEFINE FIELD embedding ON conversation TYPE array<float>;
   458→DEFINE INDEX idx_conv_embedding ON conversation FIELDS embedding
   459→  HNSW DIMENSION 1536 DIST COSINE;
   460→
   461→-- KNN search (top 5 nearest neighbors)
   462→SELECT *, vector::similarity::cosine(embedding, $query_vec) AS similarity
   463→FROM conversation
   464→WHERE embedding <|5, COSINE|> $query_vec
   465→ORDER BY similarity DESC;
   466→
   467→-- Hybrid search (vector + full-text via RRF)
   468→LET $vs = SELECT id FROM conversation WHERE embedding <|5, COSINE|> $query_vec;
   469→LET $ft = SELECT id, search::score(1) AS score FROM conversation WHERE text @1@ 'decision about auth';
   470→RETURN search::rrf([$vs, $ft], 5);
   471→```
   472→
   473→## Real-Time & Live Queries
   474→
   475→- **Live Query Streaming:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/sdk/javascript/core/streaming
   476→- **LIVE SELECT:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/live
   477→
   478→Useful for pushing graph updates to the frontend in real time (new entity extracted -> graph view updates, new conflict detected -> feed updates).
   479→
   480→## Full-Text Search
   481→
   482→- **DEFINE ANALYZER:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/analyzer
   483→- **Search Functions:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/functions/database/search
   484→
   485→## Useful Built-in Functions
   486→
   487→- **Time Functions (time::now, time::floor, etc.):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/functions/database/time
   488→- **Array Functions (array::group, array::flatten, etc.):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/functions/database/array
   489→- **String Functions:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/functions/database/string
   490→- **Record Functions (record::id, record::table):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/functions/database/record
   491→- **Math Functions:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/functions/database/math
   492→
   493→## Auth & Permissions
   494→
   495→- **Authentication Overview:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/security/authentication
   496→- **DEFINE USER:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/user
   497→- **DEFINE TOKEN:** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/statements/define/token
   498→
   499→## Tools
   500→
   501→- **Surrealist (GUI client for local inspection/debugging):** https://surrealdb.com/surrealist
   502→- **CLI Reference (surreal start, import, export):** https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealdb/cli
   503→
   504→---
   505→
   506→## Platform-Specific Patterns
   507→
   508→### Pattern: Extraction Pipeline Write
   509→After the LLM extracts entities from a message, write them to the graph in a single transaction:
   510→```sql
   511→BEGIN TRANSACTION;
   512→  -- Create entities
   513→  LET $task = CREATE task SET title = $title, status = 'open', owner = $owner;
   514→  LET $decision = CREATE decision SET summary = $summary, status = 'extracted', confidence = $conf;
   515→  
   516→  -- Create relationships
   517→  RELATE $decision->decided_in->$conversation;
   518→  RELATE $decision->belongs_to->$project;
   519→  RELATE $task->belongs_to->$feature;
   520→  RELATE $person->owns->$task;
   521→COMMIT TRANSACTION;
   522→```
   523→
   524→### Pattern: Cross-Project Conflict Detection
   525→Traverse the graph to find related entities across projects:
   526→```sql
   527→-- Find all decisions in projects that share dependencies
   528→SELECT 
   529→  id, summary, status,
   530→  <-belongs_to<-project AS projects,
   531→  ->conflicts_with->decision AS conflicts
   532→FROM decision
   533→WHERE status IN ['extracted', 'proposed', 'confirmed']
   534→  AND <-belongs_to<-project != $current_project;
   535→```
   536→
   537→### Pattern: Context Packet for MCP Server
   538→Build a token-budgeted context packet for coding agents:
   539→```sql
   540→-- Active decisions for a project
   541→SELECT summary, rationale, status, decided_at
   542→FROM decision
   543→WHERE <-belongs_to<-project = $project
   544→  AND status IN ['confirmed', 'proposed']
   545→ORDER BY decided_at DESC
   546→LIMIT 20;
   547→
   548→-- Dependency chain for current task
   549→SELECT 
   550→  id, title, status,
   551→  ->depends_on->task.{id, title, status} AS dependencies
   552→FROM task
   553→WHERE id = $current_task;
   554→```
   555→

content.content:
1→/**
     2→ * Embedded plugin content for `brain init`.
     3→ *
     4→ * The compiled binary can't read plugin/ files at runtime,
     5→ * so we embed hooks, CLAUDE.md, and commands as constants.
     6→ */
     7→
     8→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
     9→// Hooks (from plugin/hooks/hooks.json)
    10→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    11→
    12→export const BRAIN_HOOKS: Record<string, Array<{ type: string; command?: string; prompt?: string }>> = {
    13→  SessionStart: [
    14→    { type: "command", command: "brain system load-context" },
    15→  ],
    16→  UserPromptSubmit: [
    17→    { type: "command", command: "brain system check-updates" },
    18→  ],
    19→  Stop: [
    20→    {
    21→      type: "prompt",
    22→      prompt: `Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools. Check each category:
    23→
    24→1. **Decisions**: Implementation choices made but not logged. Call create_provisional_decision for each.
    25→2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
    26→3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
    27→4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
    28→5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
    29→6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).
    30→
    31→After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
    32→{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}
    33→
    34→If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
    35→{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}`,
    36→    },
    37→  ],
    38→  SessionEnd: [
    39→    { type: "command", command: "brain system end-session" },
    40→  ],
    41→};
    42→
    43→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    44→// CLAUDE.md (from plugin/CLAUDE.md)
    45→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    46→
    47→export const BRAIN_CLAUDE_MD = `# Brain Knowledge Graph Integration
    48→
    49→This project is connected to the Brain knowledge graph via MCP tools. The graph contains decisions, constraints, tasks, questions, and observations from all agents and humans working on this workspace.
    50→
    51→## Project Context
    52→
    53→At session start you receive a list of workspace projects with their IDs. Most MCP tools require a \`project_id\` — infer it from context:
    54→
    55→- **Task-scoped work**: If you're given a task ID, call \`get_entity_detail\` — the task's graph relationships reveal its project.
    56→- **Directory-scoped work**: Match the directory you're working in to a project. In a monorepo, each service/package directory typically maps to a project. In a monolith, a module or subpackage may map to a project or feature.
    57→- **Single project**: If the workspace has only one project, use it.
    58→- **Ambiguous**: If you can't determine the project, ask the user.
    59→
    60→## Hooks
    61→
    62→- **SessionStart** loads workspace info (available projects and IDs)
    63→- **UserPromptSubmit** checks for workspace-level graph updates
    64→- **Stop** catches unlogged decisions before the session ends
    65→- **SessionEnd** logs session summary to the graph
    66→
    67→## Data Model
    68→
    69→The knowledge graph has these entity types. Use this to pick the right MCP tools and understand tool results.
    70→
    71→**Work hierarchy:** Project → Feature → Task
    72→- **Project**: a named initiative with status, description. Linked to workspace via \`has_project\` edge.
    73→- **Feature**: a capability or deliverable within a project. Linked via \`has_feature\` edge.
    74→- **Task**: an actionable work item. Can belong to a feature (\`has_task\`) or directly to a project (\`belongs_to\`). Has status (open/todo/ready/in_progress/blocked/done/completed), priority, category, optional owner and deadline.
    75→
    76→**Cross-cutting entities** (attach to any level via \`belongs_to\`):
    77→- **Decision**: a choice that was made. Status lifecycle: extracted → proposed → provisional → confirmed → superseded. Can conflict with other decisions (\`conflicts_with\` edge).
    78→- **Question**: an open question requiring a choice. Only for pending decisions, not informational queries.
    79→- **Observation**: a lightweight signal (info/warning/conflict). Lifecycle: open → acknowledged → resolved. Used for cross-project intelligence.
    80→- **Suggestion**: a proactive agent-to-human proposal. Categories: optimization, risk, opportunity, conflict, missing, pivot. Lifecycle: pending → accepted/dismissed/deferred → converted.
    81→
    82→**Other entities:** Person (with ownership edges), Meeting, Document, Git Commit, Pull Request.
    83→
    84→**Key relationships (graph edges):**
    85→- \`has_project\`: workspace → project
    86→- \`has_feature\`: project → feature
    87→- \`has_task\`: feature → task
    88→- \`belongs_to\`: task|decision|question → feature|project
    89→- \`depends_on\`: task|feature → task|feature (blocks/needs/soft)
    90→- \`conflicts_with\`: decision|feature ↔ decision|feature
    91→- \`owns\`: person → task|project|feature
    92→- \`observes\`: observation → project|feature|task|decision|question
    93→- \`suggests_for\`: suggestion → project|feature|task|question|decision
    94→- \`suggestion_evidence\`: suggestion → observation|decision|task|feature|project|question|person|workspace
    95→- \`superseded_by\`: decision → decision
    96→
    97→**Entity ID format:** MCP tools use \`table:id\` for polymorphic references (e.g. \`task:abc123\`, \`decision:def456\`).
    98→
    99→## MCP Tools Available
   100→
   101→### Context (progressive detail)
   102→- \`get_workspace_context\` — Workspace overview: projects with entity counts, hot items, active sessions. Already loaded at session start.
   103→- \`get_project_context\` — Full project context: decisions, tasks, questions, observations, suggestions. Requires project_id.
   104→- \`get_task_context\` — Task-focused: subgraph (subtasks, deps, siblings) + project hot items. Requires task_id, resolves project automatically.
   105→
   106→### Read (use freely)
   107→- \`get_active_decisions\` — Decisions grouped by status (confirmed/provisional/contested)
   108→- \`get_task_dependencies\` — Dependency tree for a task (depends on, depended by, subtasks)
   109→- \`get_architecture_constraints\` — Hard and soft constraints from decisions and observations
   110→- \`get_recent_changes\` — What changed since your last session
   111→- \`get_entity_detail\` — Full detail for any entity (entity ID format: \`table:id\`)
   112→
   113→### Reason (use when making choices)
   114→- \`resolve_decision\` — Check if the graph already answers your question. **Always try this before creating a new decision.**
   115→- \`check_constraints\` — Verify a proposed action doesn't conflict with existing decisions. **Use before adding dependencies or changing approaches.**
   116→
   117→### Write (use to keep the graph current)
   118→- \`create_provisional_decision\` — Record an implementation choice you made. Status is "provisional" — only humans confirm.
   119→- \`ask_question\` — When genuinely uncertain, ask rather than guess. Creates a question for human review.
   120→- \`update_task_status\` — Track progress. Triggers automatic subtask rollup on parent tasks.
   121→- \`create_subtask\` — Break tasks into smaller pieces. Includes semantic dedup (returns existing if similar).
   122→- \`log_implementation_note\` — Append notes about what was implemented and how.
   123→- \`create_suggestion\` — Propose an optimization, risk, opportunity, conflict, missing element, or pivot for human review. Surfaces in the feed.
   124→
   125→## Decision Governance
   126→
   127→- **Your decisions are always \`provisional\` or \`inferred\`** — only humans confirm.
   128→- This means you can move fast without blocking, while humans retain authority.
   129→- Provisional decisions surface in the feed as DecisionReview cards for human approval.
   130→
   131→## Best Practices
   132→
   133→1. **Check before deciding.** Call \`resolve_decision\` first — the answer may already exist from another agent or human.
   134→2. **Ask, don't guess.** If you're uncertain, \`ask_question\` is better than \`create_provisional_decision\`. A question says "I need input." A decision says "I picked this, review it."
   135→3. **Log as you go.** Don't batch decisions for the end. Log each significant choice when you make it.
   136→4. **Decompose tasks.** Use \`create_subtask\` to break work into pieces, then update status as each completes.
   137→5. **Check constraints.** Before adding a dependency or changing an approach, call \`check_constraints\`.
   138→6. **Write descriptive commit messages and include task IDs.** Include the raw task ID(s) in the commit message to make webhook processing and follow-up linking/review unambiguous. Use a clear token like \`task:<raw-task-id>\` (or multiple, e.g. \`tasks: <id1>, <id2>\`). Describe *what* changed and *why*.`;
   139→
   140→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   141→// Commands (slash commands installed to .claude/commands/)
   142→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   143→
   144→export const BRAIN_COMMANDS: Record<string, string> = {
   145→  "brain-start-task.md": `---
   146→name: brain-start-task
   147→description: Start working on a specific task from the Brain knowledge graph
   148→user_invocable: true
   149→args: task_id
   150→---
   151→
   152→# /brain-start-task <task_id>
   153→
   154→Start a task-scoped session focused on a specific task.
   155→
   156→## Instructions
   157→
   158→1. Call \`get_project_context\` with the task_id to get task-scoped context
   159→2. Display the task details: title, description, status, dependencies
   160→3. Show existing subtasks (if a previous agent already decomposed this task — do NOT re-decompose)
   161→4. Show sibling tasks (for awareness of parallel work)
   162→5. Show related decisions and constraints
   163→6. Call \`update_task_status\` to set the task to "in_progress" if it's currently "todo" or "ready"
   164→7. If the task has no subtasks, analyze what needs to be done and offer to create subtasks via \`create_subtask\`
   165→8. Begin implementation, using Brain MCP tools throughout:
   166→   - \`resolve_decision\` when hitting implementation choices
   167→   - \`check_constraints\` before adding dependencies or changing approaches
   168→   - \`create_provisional_decision\` when making choices the graph doesn't cover
   169→   - \`ask_question\` when genuinely uncertain
   170→   - \`update_task_status\` as subtasks complete`,
   171→
   172→  "brain-status.md": `---
   173→name: brain-status
   174→description: Show current Brain knowledge graph context for this project
   175→user_invocable: true
   176→---
   177→
   178→# /brain-status
   179→
   180→Show the current project context from the Brain knowledge graph.
   181→
   182→## Instructions
   183→
   184→1. Call the \`get_project_context\` MCP tool with the current project ID
   185→2. Display the results in a readable format:
   186→   - Contested decisions (conflicts that need resolution)
   187→   - Confirmed decisions (follow these)
   188→   - Provisional decisions (follow but note for review)
   189→   - Active tasks with status
   190→   - Open questions
   191→   - Recent changes
   192→3. If there are contested decisions, highlight them prominently
   193→4. Suggest next actions based on the context (e.g., "Task X is blocked by question Y")`,
   194→};
   195→

   191→      },

   194→          mcpHandlers.handleGetChanges(request.params.workspaceId, request),

   196→      },

   199→          mcpHandlers.handleGetEntityDetail(request.params.workspaceId, request.params.entityId, request),
   200→        ),

   210→          mcpHandlers.handleCheckConstraints(request.params.workspaceId, request),
   211→        ),
   212→      },
   213→      // MCP — Tier 3 Write
   214→      "/api/mcp/:workspaceId/decisions/provisional": {
   215→        POST: withRequestLogging("POST /api/mcp/:workspaceId/decisions/provisional", "POST", (request) =>

content.text:
You are designing a feature for the Brain knowledge graph CLI at /Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/amman-v1.

## Background

The Brain CLI (`cli/brain.ts`) connects coding agents to a knowledge graph via MCP tools. The `brain init` command writes CLAUDE.md content (between `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers) that teaches agents how to use the graph.

Claude Code has hierarchical CLAUDE.md loading:
- **Ancestor loading**: walks upward from cwd to root, loading every CLAUDE.md immediately
- **Descendant loading**: lazy-loads CLAUDE.md in subdirectories only when files in those dirs are accessed

## Feature: `brain map` — Directory-to-Graph Entity Mapping

The idea: let users map directories to brain graph entities (project, feature) by writing per-directory CLAUDE.md files. When a coding agent enters that directory, it automatically knows which entity it's working in and uses the right MCP tool parameters.

**The graph never stores directory paths.** Directories point at graph entities. The mapping lives entirely in the filesystem.

## Existing Patterns to Reuse

1. **CLI routing** (`cli/brain.ts`): Simple switch statement, add `case "map":` dispatching to a new command module
2. **Config loading** (`cli/config.ts`): `requireConfig()` returns `{ server_url, workspace, api_key }` 
3. **HTTP client** (`cli/http-client.ts`): `getEntityDetail(entityId: string)` validates entity existence, returns entity with relationships. Takes polymorphic `table:id` format.
4. **Marker-based CLAUDE.md writing** (`cli/commands/init.ts`): Uses `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers for idempotent updates. Same pattern should be used with different markers like `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->`
5. **Init content** (`cli/commands/init-content.ts`): Templates stored as string constants

## Requirements

1. `brain map <dir> <type>:<id>` — Map a directory to a brain entity
   - Supported types: `project`, `feature` (possibly `task`)
   - Validates entity exists via HTTP client
   - Gets entity display name for the CLAUDE.md header
   - For features: auto-resolves parent project from graph relationships
   - Writes/updates CLAUDE.md in target directory with markers
   
2. `brain unmap <dir>` — Remove mapping from a directory's CLAUDE.md

3. CLAUDE.md content should be:
   - **Minimal**: just entity reference + MCP tool instructions (not stale data)
   - **Composable**: feature dir inherits project dir's context via ancestor loading
   - **Idempotent**: re-running updates existing content between markers

## Design the implementation plan

Consider:
- File structure: new `cli/commands/map.ts` module
- CLAUDE.md template content for each entity type (project vs feature vs task)
- How to resolve feature → parent project from entity detail response
- Error handling (entity not found, entity not in workspace, directory doesn't exist)
- Whether to add the template to init-content.ts or keep it in map.ts
- How `brain map` and `brain unmap` are invoked and what they output
- What the generated CLAUDE.md content should say to agents

Return a detailed implementation plan with file paths, function signatures, and content templates.

- **Ancestor loading**: walks upward from cwd to root, loading every CLAUDE.md immediately
- **Descendant loading**: lazy-loads CLAUDE.md in subdirectories only when files in those dirs are accessed

## Feature: `brain map` — Directory-to-Graph Entity Mapping

The idea: let users map directories to brain graph entities (project, feature) by writing per-directory CLAUDE.md files. When a coding agent enters that directory, it automatically knows which entity it's working in and uses the right MCP tool parameters.

**The graph never stores directory paths.** Directories point at graph entities. The mapping lives entirely in the filesystem.

## Existing Patterns to Reuse

1. **CLI routing** (`cli/brain.ts`): Simple switch statement, add `case "map":` dispatching to a new command module
2. **Config loading** (`cli/config.ts`): `requireConfig()` returns `{ server_url, workspace, api_key }` 
3. **HTTP client** (`cli/http-client.ts`): `getEntityDetail(entityId: string)` validates entity existence, returns entity with relationships. Takes polymorphic `table:id` format.
4. **Marker-based CLAUDE.md writing** (`cli/commands/init.ts`): Uses `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers for idempotent updates. Same pattern should be used with different markers like `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->`
5. **Init content** (`cli/commands/init-content.ts`): Templates stored as string constants

## Requirements

1. `brain map <dir> <type>:<id>` — Map a directory to a brain entity
   - Supported types: `project`, `feature` (possibly `task`)
   - Validates entity exists via HTTP client
   - Gets entity display name for the CLAUDE.md header
   - For features: auto-resolves parent project from graph relationships
   - Writes/updates CLAUDE.md in target directory with markers
   
2. `brain unmap <dir>` — Remove mapping from a directory's CLAUDE.md

3. CLAUDE.md content should be:
   - **Minimal**: just entity reference + MCP tool instructions (not stale data)
   - **Composable**: feature dir inherits project dir's context via ancestor loading
   - **Idempotent**: re-running updates existing content between markers

## Design the implementation plan

Consider:
- File structure: new `cli/commands/map.ts` module
- CLAUDE.md template content for each entity type (project vs feature vs task)
- How to resolve feature → parent project from entity detail response
- Error handling (entity not found, entity not in workspace, directory doesn't exist)
- Whether to add the template to init-content.ts or keep it in map.ts
- How `brain map` and `brain unmap` are invoked and what they output
- What the generated CLAUDE.md content should say to agents

Return a detailed implementation plan with file paths, function signatures, and content templates.

content.content:
1→/**
     2→ * Embedded plugin content for `brain init`.
     3→ *
     4→ * The compiled binary can't read plugin/ files at runtime,
     5→ * so we embed hooks, CLAUDE.md, and commands as constants.
     6→ */
     7→
     8→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
     9→// Hooks (from plugin/hooks/hooks.json)
    10→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    11→
    12→export const BRAIN_HOOKS: Record<string, Array<{ type: string; command?: string; prompt?: string }>> = {
    13→  SessionStart: [
    14→    { type: "command", command: "brain system load-context" },
    15→  ],
    16→  UserPromptSubmit: [
    17→    { type: "command", command: "brain system check-updates" },
    18→  ],
    19→  Stop: [
    20→    {
    21→      type: "prompt",
    22→      prompt: `Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools. Check each category:
    23→
    24→1. **Decisions**: Implementation choices made but not logged. Call create_provisional_decision for each.
    25→2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
    26→3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
    27→4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
    28→5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
    29→6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).
    30→
    31→After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
    32→{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}
    33→
    34→If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
    35→{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}`,
    36→    },
    37→  ],
    38→  SessionEnd: [
    39→    { type: "command", command: "brain system end-session" },
    40→  ],
    41→};
    42→
    43→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    44→// CLAUDE.md (from plugin/CLAUDE.md)
    45→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    46→
    47→export const BRAIN_CLAUDE_MD = `# Brain Knowledge Graph Integration
    48→
    49→This project is connected to the Brain knowledge graph via MCP tools. The graph contains decisions, constraints, tasks, questions, and observations from all agents and humans working on this workspace.
    50→
    51→## Project Context
    52→
    53→At session start you receive a list of workspace projects with their IDs. Most MCP tools require a \`project_id\` — infer it from context:
    54→
    55→- **Task-scoped work**: If you're given a task ID, call \`get_entity_detail\` — the task's graph relationships reveal its project.
    56→- **Directory-scoped work**: Match the directory you're working in to a project. In a monorepo, each service/package directory typically maps to a project. In a monolith, a module or subpackage may map to a project or feature.
    57→- **Single project**: If the workspace has only one project, use it.
    58→- **Ambiguous**: If you can't determine the project, ask the user.
    59→
    60→## Hooks
    61→
    62→- **SessionStart** loads workspace info (available projects and IDs)
    63→- **UserPromptSubmit** checks for workspace-level graph updates
    64→- **Stop** catches unlogged decisions before the session ends
    65→- **SessionEnd** logs session summary to the graph
    66→
    67→## Data Model
    68→
    69→The knowledge graph has these entity types. Use this to pick the right MCP tools and understand tool results.
    70→
    71→**Work hierarchy:** Project → Feature → Task
    72→- **Project**: a named initiative with status, description. Linked to workspace via \`has_project\` edge.
    73→- **Feature**: a capability or deliverable within a project. Linked via \`has_feature\` edge.
    74→- **Task**: an actionable work item. Can belong to a feature (\`has_task\`) or directly to a project (\`belongs_to\`). Has status (open/todo/ready/in_progress/blocked/done/completed), priority, category, optional owner and deadline.
    75→
    76→**Cross-cutting entities** (attach to any level via \`belongs_to\`):
    77→- **Decision**: a choice that was made. Status lifecycle: extracted → proposed → provisional → confirmed → superseded. Can conflict with other decisions (\`conflicts_with\` edge).
    78→- **Question**: an open question requiring a choice. Only for pending decisions, not informational queries.
    79→- **Observation**: a lightweight signal (info/warning/conflict). Lifecycle: open → acknowledged → resolved. Used for cross-project intelligence.
    80→- **Suggestion**: a proactive agent-to-human proposal. Categories: optimization, risk, opportunity, conflict, missing, pivot. Lifecycle: pending → accepted/dismissed/deferred → converted.
    81→
    82→**Other entities:** Person (with ownership edges), Meeting, Document, Git Commit, Pull Request.
    83→
    84→**Key relationships (graph edges):**
    85→- \`has_project\`: workspace → project
    86→- \`has_feature\`: project → feature
    87→- \`has_task\`: feature → task
    88→- \`belongs_to\`: task|decision|question → feature|project
    89→- \`depends_on\`: task|feature → task|feature (blocks/needs/soft)
    90→- \`conflicts_with\`: decision|feature ↔ decision|feature
    91→- \`owns\`: person → task|project|feature
    92→- \`observes\`: observation → project|feature|task|decision|question
    93→- \`suggests_for\`: suggestion → project|feature|task|question|decision
    94→- \`suggestion_evidence\`: suggestion → observation|decision|task|feature|project|question|person|workspace
    95→- \`superseded_by\`: decision → decision
    96→
    97→**Entity ID format:** MCP tools use \`table:id\` for polymorphic references (e.g. \`task:abc123\`, \`decision:def456\`).
    98→
    99→## MCP Tools Available
   100→
   101→### Context (progressive detail)
   102→- \`get_workspace_context\` — Workspace overview: projects with entity counts, hot items, active sessions. Already loaded at session start.
   103→- \`get_project_context\` — Full project context: decisions, tasks, questions, observations, suggestions. Requires project_id.
   104→- \`get_task_context\` — Task-focused: subgraph (subtasks, deps, siblings) + project hot items. Requires task_id, resolves project automatically.
   105→
   106→### Read (use freely)
   107→- \`get_active_decisions\` — Decisions grouped by status (confirmed/provisional/contested)
   108→- \`get_task_dependencies\` — Dependency tree for a task (depends on, depended by, subtasks)
   109→- \`get_architecture_constraints\` — Hard and soft constraints from decisions and observations
   110→- \`get_recent_changes\` — What changed since your last session
   111→- \`get_entity_detail\` — Full detail for any entity (entity ID format: \`table:id\`)
   112→
   113→### Reason (use when making choices)
   114→- \`resolve_decision\` — Check if the graph already answers your question. **Always try this before creating a new decision.**
   115→- \`check_constraints\` — Verify a proposed action doesn't conflict with existing decisions. **Use before adding dependencies or changing approaches.**
   116→
   117→### Write (use to keep the graph current)
   118→- \`create_provisional_decision\` — Record an implementation choice you made. Status is "provisional" — only humans confirm.
   119→- \`ask_question\` — When genuinely uncertain, ask rather than guess. Creates a question for human review.
   120→- \`update_task_status\` — Track progress. Triggers automatic subtask rollup on parent tasks.
   121→- \`create_subtask\` — Break tasks into smaller pieces. Includes semantic dedup (returns existing if similar).
   122→- \`log_implementation_note\` — Append notes about what was implemented and how.
   123→- \`create_suggestion\` — Propose an optimization, risk, opportunity, conflict, missing element, or pivot for human review. Surfaces in the feed.
   124→
   125→## Decision Governance
   126→
   127→- **Your decisions are always \`provisional\` or \`inferred\`** — only humans confirm.
   128→- This means you can move fast without blocking, while humans retain authority.
   129→- Provisional decisions surface in the feed as DecisionReview cards for human approval.
   130→
   131→## Best Practices
   132→
   133→1. **Check before deciding.** Call \`resolve_decision\` first — the answer may already exist from another agent or human.
   134→2. **Ask, don't guess.** If you're uncertain, \`ask_question\` is better than \`create_provisional_decision\`. A question says "I need input." A decision says "I picked this, review it."
   135→3. **Log as you go.** Don't batch decisions for the end. Log each significant choice when you make it.
   136→4. **Decompose tasks.** Use \`create_subtask\` to break work into pieces, then update status as each completes.
   137→5. **Check constraints.** Before adding a dependency or changing an approach, call \`check_constraints\`.
   138→6. **Write descriptive commit messages and include task IDs.** Include the raw task ID(s) in the commit message to make webhook processing and follow-up linking/review unambiguous. Use a clear token like \`task:<raw-task-id>\` (or multiple, e.g. \`tasks: <id1>, <id2>\`). Describe *what* changed and *why*.`;
   139→
   140→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   141→// Commands (slash commands installed to .claude/commands/)
   142→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   143→
   144→export const BRAIN_COMMANDS: Record<string, string> = {
   145→  "brain-start-task.md": `---
   146→name: brain-start-task
   147→description: Start working on a specific task from the Brain knowledge graph
   148→user_invocable: true
   149→args: task_id
   150→---
   151→
   152→# /brain-start-task <task_id>
   153→
   154→Start a task-scoped session focused on a specific task.
   155→
   156→## Instructions
   157→
   158→1. Call \`get_project_context\` with the task_id to get task-scoped context
   159→2. Display the task details: title, description, status, dependencies
   160→3. Show existing subtasks (if a previous agent already decomposed this task — do NOT re-decompose)
   161→4. Show sibling tasks (for awareness of parallel work)
   162→5. Show related decisions and constraints
   163→6. Call \`update_task_status\` to set the task to "in_progress" if it's currently "todo" or "ready"
   164→7. If the task has no subtasks, analyze what needs to be done and offer to create subtasks via \`create_subtask\`
   165→8. Begin implementation, using Brain MCP tools throughout:
   166→   - \`resolve_decision\` when hitting implementation choices
   167→   - \`check_constraints\` before adding dependencies or changing approaches
   168→   - \`create_provisional_decision\` when making choices the graph doesn't cover
   169→   - \`ask_question\` when genuinely uncertain
   170→   - \`update_task_status\` as subtasks complete`,
   171→
   172→  "brain-status.md": `---
   173→name: brain-status
   174→description: Show current Brain knowledge graph context for this project
   175→user_invocable: true
   176→---
   177→
   178→# /brain-status
   179→
   180→Show the current project context from the Brain knowledge graph.
   181→
   182→## Instructions
   183→
   184→1. Call the \`get_project_context\` MCP tool with the current project ID
   185→2. Display the results in a readable format:
   186→   - Contested decisions (conflicts that need resolution)
   187→   - Confirmed decisions (follow these)
   188→   - Provisional decisions (follow but note for review)
   189→   - Active tasks with status
   190→   - Open questions
   191→   - Recent changes
   192→3. If there are contested decisions, highlight them prominently
   193→4. Suggest next actions based on the context (e.g., "Task X is blocked by question Y")`,
   194→};
   195→

You are designing a feature for the Brain knowledge graph CLI at /Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/amman-v1.

## Background

The Brain CLI (`cli/brain.ts`) connects coding agents to a knowledge graph via MCP tools. The `brain init` command writes CLAUDE.md content (between `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers) that teaches agents how to use the graph.

Claude Code has hierarchical CLAUDE.md loading:
- **Ancestor loading**: walks upward from cwd to root, loading every CLAUDE.md immediately
- **Descendant loading**: lazy-loads CLAUDE.md in subdirectories only when files in those dirs are accessed

## Feature: `brain map` — Directory-to-Graph Entity Mapping

The idea: let users map directories to brain graph entities (project, feature) by writing per-directory CLAUDE.md files. When a coding agent enters that directory, it automatically knows which entity it's working in and uses the right MCP tool parameters.

**The graph never stores directory paths.** Directories point at graph entities. The mapping lives entirely in the filesystem.

## Existing Patterns to Reuse

1. **CLI routing** (`cli/brain.ts`): Simple switch statement, add `case "map":` dispatching to a new command module
2. **Config loading** (`cli/config.ts`): `requireConfig()` returns `{ server_url, workspace, api_key }` 
3. **HTTP client** (`cli/http-client.ts`): `getEntityDetail(entityId: string)` validates entity existence, returns entity with relationships. Takes polymorphic `table:id` format.
4. **Marker-based CLAUDE.md writing** (`cli/commands/init.ts`): Uses `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers for idempotent updates. Same pattern should be used with different markers like `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->`
5. **Init content** (`cli/commands/init-content.ts`): Templates stored as string constants

## Requirements

1. `brain map <dir> <type>:<id>` — Map a directory to a brain entity
   - Supported types: `project`, `feature` (possibly `task`)
   - Validates entity exists via HTTP client
   - Gets entity display name for the CLAUDE.md header
   - For features: auto-resolves parent project from graph relationships
   - Writes/updates CLAUDE.md in target directory with markers
   
2. `brain unmap <dir>` — Remove mapping from a directory's CLAUDE.md

3. CLAUDE.md content should be:
   - **Minimal**: just entity reference + MCP tool instructions (not stale data)
   - **Composable**: feature dir inherits project dir's context via ancestor loading
   - **Idempotent**: re-running updates existing content between markers

## Design the implementation plan

Consider:
- File structure: new `cli/commands/map.ts` module
- CLAUDE.md template content for each entity type (project vs feature vs task)
- How to resolve feature → parent project from entity detail response
- Error handling (entity not found, entity not in workspace, directory doesn't exist)
- Whether to add the template to init-content.ts or keep it in map.ts
- How `brain map` and `brain unmap` are invoked and what they output
- What the generated CLAUDE.md content should say to agents

Return a detailed implementation plan with file paths, function signatures, and content templates.

You are designing a feature for the Brain knowledge graph CLI at /Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/amman-v1.

## Background

The Brain CLI (`cli/brain.ts`) connects coding agents to a knowledge graph via MCP tools. The `brain init` command writes CLAUDE.md content (between `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers) that teaches agents how to use the graph.

Claude Code has hierarchical CLAUDE.md loading:
- **Ancestor loading**: walks upward from cwd to root, loading every CLAUDE.md immediately
- **Descendant loading**: lazy-loads CLAUDE.md in subdirectories only when files in those dirs are accessed

## Feature: `brain map` — Directory-to-Graph Entity Mapping

The idea: let users map directories to brain graph entities (project, feature) by writing per-directory CLAUDE.md files. When a coding agent enters that directory, it automatically knows which entity it's working in and uses the right MCP tool parameters.

**The graph never stores directory paths.** Directories point at graph entities. The mapping lives entirely in the filesystem.

## Existing Patterns to Reuse

1. **CLI routing** (`cli/brain.ts`): Simple switch statement, add `case "map":` dispatching to a new command module
2. **Config loading** (`cli/config.ts`): `requireConfig()` returns `{ server_url, workspace, api_key }` 
3. **HTTP client** (`cli/http-client.ts`): `getEntityDetail(entityId: string)` validates entity existence, returns entity with relationships. Takes polymorphic `table:id` format.
4. **Marker-based CLAUDE.md writing** (`cli/commands/init.ts`): Uses `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers for idempotent updates. Same pattern should be used with different markers like `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->`
5. **Init content** (`cli/commands/init-content.ts`): Templates stored as string constants

## Requirements

1. `brain map <dir> <type>:<id>` — Map a directory to a brain entity
   - Supported types: `project`, `feature` (possibly `task`)
   - Validates entity exists via HTTP client
   - Gets entity display name for the CLAUDE.md header
   - For features: auto-resolves parent project from graph relationships
   - Writes/updates CLAUDE.md in target directory with markers
   
2. `brain unmap <dir>` — Remove mapping from a directory's CLAUDE.md

3. CLAUDE.md content should be:
   - **Minimal**: just entity reference + MCP tool instructions (not stale data)
   - **Composable**: feature dir inherits project dir's context via ancestor loading
   - **Idempotent**: re-running updates existing content between markers

## Design the implementation plan

Consider:
- File structure: new `cli/commands/map.ts` module
- CLAUDE.md template content for each entity type (project vs feature vs task)
- How to resolve feature → parent project from entity detail response
- Error handling (entity not found, entity not in workspace, directory doesn't exist)
- Whether to add the template to init-content.ts or keep it in map.ts
- How `brain map` and `brain unmap` are invoked and what they output
- What the generated CLAUDE.md content should say to agents

Return a detailed implementation plan with file paths, function signatures, and content templates.

## Background

The Brain CLI (`cli/brain.ts`) connects coding agents to a knowledge graph via MCP tools. The `brain init` command writes CLAUDE.md content (between `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers) that teaches agents how to use the graph.

Claude Code has hierarchical CLAUDE.md loading:
- **Ancestor loading**: walks upward from cwd to root, loading every CLAUDE.md immediately
- **Descendant loading**: lazy-loads CLAUDE.md in subdirectories only when files in those dirs are accessed

## Feature: `brain map` — Directory-to-Graph Entity Mapping

The idea: let users map directories to brain graph entities (project, feature) by writing per-directory CLAUDE.md files. When a coding agent enters that directory, it automatically knows which entity it's working in and uses the right MCP tool parameters.

**The graph never stores directory paths.** Directories point at graph entities. The mapping lives entirely in the filesystem.

## Existing Patterns to Reuse

1. **CLI routing** (`cli/brain.ts`): Simple switch statement, add `case "map":` dispatching to a new command module
2. **Config loading** (`cli/config.ts`): `requireConfig()` returns `{ server_url, workspace, api_key }` 
3. **HTTP client** (`cli/http-client.ts`): `getEntityDetail(entityId: string)` validates entity existence, returns entity with relationships. Takes polymorphic `table:id` format.
4. **Marker-based CLAUDE.md writing** (`cli/commands/init.ts`): Uses `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers for idempotent updates. Same pattern should be used with different markers like `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->`
5. **Init content** (`cli/commands/init-content.ts`): Templates stored as string constants

## Requirements

1. `brain map <dir> <type>:<id>` — Map a directory to a brain entity
   - Supported types: `project`, `feature` (possibly `task`)
   - Validates entity exists via HTTP client
   - Gets entity display name for the CLAUDE.md header
   - For features: auto-resolves parent project from graph relationships
   - Writes/updates CLAUDE.md in target directory with markers
   
2. `brain unmap <dir>` — Remove mapping from a directory's CLAUDE.md

3. CLAUDE.md content should be:
   - **Minimal**: just entity reference + MCP tool instructions (not stale data)
   - **Composable**: feature dir inherits project dir's context via ancestor loading
   - **Idempotent**: re-running updates existing content between markers

## Design the implementation plan

Consider:
- File structure: new `cli/commands/map.ts` module
- CLAUDE.md template content for each entity type (project vs feature vs task)
- How to resolve feature → parent project from entity detail response
- Error handling (entity not found, entity not in workspace, directory doesn't exist)
- Whether to add the template to init-content.ts or keep it in map.ts
- How `brain map` and `brain unmap` are invoked and what they output
- What the generated CLAUDE.md content should say to agents

Return a detailed implementation plan with file paths, function signatures, and content templates.

input.thought:
Let me synthesize everything I've learned into the implementation plan.

## Key findings:

1. **CLI entry point** (`cli/brain.ts`): Simple switch dispatch, needs `case "map":` and `case "unmap":` additions.

2. **Config pattern**: `requireConfig()` returns `{ server_url, workspace, api_key }`. All commands use this.

3. **HTTP client**: `getEntityDetail(entityId: string)` takes `table:id` format (e.g., `project:abc123`, `feature:def456`). Returns `EntityDetailResponse` with:
   - `entity: { id, kind, name, data }`
   - `relationships: Array<{ id, kind, name, relationKind, direction, confidence }>`
   - `provenance: Array<...>`

4. **Relationship structure for parent resolution**:
   - For a feature, the parent project appears in `relationships` as: `{ kind: "project", relationKind: "has_feature", direction: "incoming" }`
   - For a task, the parent feature appears as: `{ kind: "feature", relationKind: "has_task", direction: "incoming" }`
   - A task could also have `{ kind: "project" | "feature", relationKind: "belongs_to", direction: "outgoing" }`

5. **Marker-based CLAUDE.md writing** (`init.ts` lines 141-166):
   - Markers: `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->`
   - Uses string indexOf to find existing markers, replaces content between them
   - For new files, appends with separator
   - Pattern is clean and should be reused with different markers: `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->`

6. **Content templates** (`init-content.ts`): String constants for CLAUDE.md, hooks, commands. The map templates could either go here or in a new `map-content.ts`.

## Design decisions:

**File structure:**
- `cli/commands/map.ts` — main module for both `brain map` and `brain unmap`
- Template content directly in `map.ts` (not `init-content.ts`) since map is a distinct concern

**Entity type resolution for features → parent project:**
From the entity detail response, for a `feature:id`:
```
relationships.find(r => r.kind === "project" && r.relationKind === "has_feature" && r.direction === "incoming")
```

For a `task:id`:
```
// Task could be under a feature (has_task) or directly under a project (belongs_to)
relationships.find(r => r.kind === "feature" && r.relationKind === "has_task" && r.direction === "incoming")
// or
relationships.find(r => (r.kind === "project" || r.kind === "feature") && r.relationKind === "belongs_to" && r.direction === "outgoing")
```

**CLAUDE.md content per entity type:**

For **project**:
```markdown
<!-- brain-map-start -->
# Brain: Project — "My Project"
This directory is mapped to project `project:uuid` in the Brain knowledge graph.
When using Brain MCP tools in this directory, use `project_id: "uuid"` for project-scoped tools.
<!-- brain-map-end -->
```

For **feature**:
```markdown
<!-- brain-map-start -->
# Brain: Feature — "Auth System" (project: "My Project")
This directory is mapped to feature `feature:uuid` in the Brain knowledge graph.
When using Brain MCP tools in this directory:
- Use `project_id: "parent-project-uuid"` for project-scoped tools
- Use `feature: "uuid"` for feature context parameters
<!-- brain-map-end -->
```

For **task**:
```markdown
<!-- brain-map-start -->
# Brain: Task — "Implement login" (project: "My Project")
This directory is mapped to task `task:uuid` in the Brain knowledge graph.
When using Brain MCP tools in this directory:
- Use `task_id: "uuid"` for task-scoped tools (get_task_context, update_task_status)
- Use `project_id: "parent-project-uuid"` for project-scoped tools
<!-- brain-map-end -->
```

The key insight is that the CLAUDE.md should tell agents **which tool parameters to use** — not cache entity data. The graph is the source of truth.

**Composability via ancestor loading:**
If a monorepo has:
```
repo/
├── CLAUDE.md  (brain init — general instructions)
├── services/
│   ├── auth/
│   │   └── CLAUDE.md  (brain map . project:auth-project-id)
│   │   └── src/
│   │       └── oauth/
│   │           └── CLAUDE.md  (brain map . feature:oauth-feature-id)
```

When an agent enters `services/auth/src/oauth/`, it loads:
1. Root CLAUDE.md (Brain plugin instructions, MCP tools)
2. `services/auth/CLAUDE.md` (project scope)
3. `services/auth/src/oauth/CLAUDE.md` (feature scope, inherits project from ancestor)

This means feature CLAUDE.md content should mention the parent project for completeness (since the agent may not have loaded the ancestor yet via lazy loading), but also be aware that an ancestor may have already set the project context.

## Background

The Brain CLI (`cli/brain.ts`) connects coding agents to a knowledge graph via MCP tools. The `brain init` command writes CLAUDE.md content (between `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers) that teaches agents how to use the graph.

Claude Code has hierarchical CLAUDE.md loading:
- **Ancestor loading**: walks upward from cwd to root, loading every CLAUDE.md immediately
- **Descendant loading**: lazy-loads CLAUDE.md in subdirectories only when files in those dirs are accessed

## Feature: `brain map` — Directory-to-Graph Entity Mapping

The idea: let users map directories to brain graph entities (project, feature) by writing per-directory CLAUDE.md files. When a coding agent enters that directory, it automatically knows which entity it's working in and uses the right MCP tool parameters.

**The graph never stores directory paths.** Directories point at graph entities. The mapping lives entirely in the filesystem.

## Existing Patterns to Reuse

1. **CLI routing** (`cli/brain.ts`): Simple switch statement, add `case "map":` dispatching to a new command module
2. **Config loading** (`cli/config.ts`): `requireConfig()` returns `{ server_url, workspace, api_key }` 
3. **HTTP client** (`cli/http-client.ts`): `getEntityDetail(entityId: string)` validates entity existence, returns entity with relationships. Takes polymorphic `table:id` format.
4. **Marker-based CLAUDE.md writing** (`cli/commands/init.ts`): Uses `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers for idempotent updates. Same pattern should be used with different markers like `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->`
5. **Init content** (`cli/commands/init-content.ts`): Templates stored as string constants

## Requirements

1. `brain map <dir> <type>:<id>` — Map a directory to a brain entity
   - Supported types: `project`, `feature` (possibly `task`)
   - Validates entity exists via HTTP client
   - Gets entity display name for the CLAUDE.md header
   - For features: auto-resolves parent project from graph relationships
   - Writes/updates CLAUDE.md in target directory with markers
   
2. `brain unmap <dir>` — Remove mapping from a directory's CLAUDE.md

3. CLAUDE.md content should be:
   - **Minimal**: just entity reference + MCP tool instructions (not stale data)
   - **Composable**: feature dir inherits project dir's context via ancestor loading
   - **Idempotent**: re-running updates existing content between markers

## Design the implementation plan

Consider:
- File structure: new `cli/commands/map.ts` module
- CLAUDE.md template content for each entity type (project vs feature vs task)
- How to resolve feature → parent project from entity detail response
- Error handling (entity not found, entity not in workspace, directory doesn't exist)
- Whether to add the template to init-content.ts or keep it in map.ts
- How `brain map` and `brain unmap` are invoked and what they output
- What the generated CLAUDE.md content should say to agents

Return a detailed implementation plan with file paths, function signatures, and content templates.

1. **CLI routing** (`cli/brain.ts`): Simple switch statement, add `case "map":` dispatching to a new command module
2. **Config loading** (`cli/config.ts`): `requireConfig()` returns `{ server_url, workspace, api_key }` 
3. **HTTP client** (`cli/http-client.ts`): `getEntityDetail(entityId: string)` validates entity existence, returns entity with relationships. Takes polymorphic `table:id` format.
4. **Marker-based CLAUDE.md writing** (`cli/commands/init.ts`): Uses `<!-- brain-plugin-start -->` / `<!-- brain-plugin-end -->` markers for idempotent updates. Same pattern should be used with different markers like `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->`
5. **Init content** (`cli/commands/init-content.ts`): Templates stored as string constants

message.content:
"The idea: map directories to brain graph entities by writing per-directory CLAUDE.md files. When a coding agent enters a directory, it automatically knows which entity it’s working in. The graph never stores paths — directories point at graph entities. The mapping lives entirely in the filesystem."

should we then also instruct it to load the project / feature context?

content.thinking:
The user is asking whether the CLAUDE.md template should also instruct the agent to automatically load project/feature context when it enters the directory — not just tell it which IDs to use, but actually trigger a context load.

This is a good question. Right now the templates just say "use these tool parameters." But the real power would be if entering a directory automatically loads the relevant context, similar to how `brain system load-context` loads workspace context at session start.

There are a few ways to think about this:

1. **CLAUDE.md instructions only**: The template tells the agent "when working in this directory, call `get_project_context` with this ID." The agent decides when to call it.


2. **Stronger instruction**: The template could say "Before starting work in this directory, call `get_project_context`/`get_task_context` to load current state" — more proactive than leaving it to the agent's discretion.
3. **Hook-based approach**: I'm considering whether a hook could fire when Claude Code reads files in a directory, but Claude Code doesn't have per-directory hooks — only event-based ones like SessionStart or UserPromptSubmit.

The real constraint is that CLAUDE.md content is just instructions — it can't execute tool calls directly, only tell the agent to do so. The lazy loading happens when Claude Code reads files in the directory, but that's still just triggering the instruction, not the actual context load.

So the key question becomes whether the template should strongly instruct "load context immediately" versus just "use these IDs when needed." I think it should be the former — the whole point of mapping directories to project contexts is that entering a directory automatically gives you the right context. Just having IDs without loading them is only half the value. The template should explicitly say something like "When you first access files in this directory, call `get_project_context` with the project ID to load current decisions, tasks, and constraints before proceeding." That way the directory mapping becomes a genuine context-loading trigger, not just parameter hints.

content.text:
Yes — that's the whole point. Just having IDs is a parameter hint. Actually loading context is what makes it useful.

The template should instruct: "When you first work in this directory, call `get_project_context` / `get_task_context` to load current decisions, tasks, and constraints."

That way entering a directory = context loads automatically (via the agent following CLAUDE.md instructions). It mirrors what `brain system load-context` does at session start, but scoped to the entity.

input.old_string:
**Project:**
```markdown
# Brain: Project — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain project `{id}`.
- `get_project_context` with `project_id: "{id}"` for decisions, tasks, constraints
- `check_constraints` with `project: "{id}"` before architectural changes
- Scope `create_provisional_decision` / `ask_question` with `context: { project: "{id}" }`
```

**Feature** (with optional `--project`):
```markdown
# Brain: Feature — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain feature `{id}`.
[if --project: Parent project: `{project_id}` — use as `project_id` for project-scoped tools.]
[else: Resolve parent project via `get_entity_detail` with `entity_id: "feature:{id}"`.]
- Scope decisions/questions with `context: { feature: "{id}" }`
```

**Task** (with optional `--project`):
```markdown
# Brain: Task — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain task `{id}`.
- `get_task_context` with `task_id: "{id}"` — auto-resolves project and loads full subgraph
- `update_task_status` with `task_id: "{id}"` to track progress
- `create_subtask` with `parent_task_id: "{id}"` to decompose work
```
input.new_string:
**Project:**
```markdown
# Brain: Project — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain project `{id}`.

**On first access**: Call `get_project_context` with `project_id: "{id}"` to load current decisions, active tasks, open questions, observations, and constraints before proceeding.

When using Brain MCP tools from this directory:
- Pass `project_id: "{id}"` to project-scoped tools (`get_active_decisions`, `get_architecture_constraints`, `get_recent_changes`)
- Pass `project: "{id}"` to `check_constraints` before architectural changes
- Scope `create_provisional_decision` / `ask_question` with `context: { project: "{id}" }`
```

**Feature** (with optional `--project`):
```markdown
# Brain: Feature — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain feature `{id}`.
[if --project: Parent project: `{project_id}`.]

**On first access**: Call `get_project_context` with `project_id: "{project_id}"` to load project-level decisions, tasks, and constraints. [if no --project: First resolve the parent project via `get_entity_detail` with `entity_id: "feature:{id}"`, then call `get_project_context` with the resolved project ID.]

When using Brain MCP tools from this directory:
- Scope decisions/questions with `context: { feature: "{id}"[, project: "{project_id}"] }`
```

**Task** (with optional `--project`):
```markdown
# Brain: Task — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain task `{id}`.

**On first access**: Call `get_task_context` with `task_id: "{id}"` to load the full task subgraph (subtasks, dependencies, siblings, parent feature) and project hot items. This auto-resolves the parent project.

When using Brain MCP tools from this directory:
- `update_task_status` with `task_id: "{id}"` to track progress
- `create_subtask` with `parent_task_id: "{id}"` to decompose work
[if --project: - Pass `project_id: "{project_id}"` to project-scoped tools]
```

# Plan: `brain map` — Directory-to-Graph Entity Mapping

## Context

The Brain CLI connects coding agents to a knowledge graph via MCP tools. Claude Code has hierarchical CLAUDE.md loading: ancestor files load immediately (upward from cwd), descendant files lazy-load when files in those dirs are accessed.

The idea: map directories to brain graph entities by writing per-directory CLAUDE.md files. When a coding agent enters a directory, it automatically knows which entity it's working in. **The graph never stores paths** — directories point at graph entities. The mapping lives entirely in the filesystem.

```
my-app/
├── CLAUDE.md                  # brain map project:<id>
├── auth/
│   ├── CLAUDE.md              # brain map feature:<id>
│   └── src/...
├── billing/
│   ├── CLAUDE.md              # brain map feature:<id>
│   └── src/...
```

## Changes

### 1. New file: `cli/commands/map.ts`

Two exports: `runMap()` and `runUnmap()`.

**`runMap()`** — `brain map <dir> <type:id> [--project <id>]`

1. Parse args: directory path, entity ref (`project:abc`, `feature:def`, `task:ghi`)
2. Validate directory exists
3. Parse entity type + raw ID (supported: `project`, `feature`, `task`)
4. Load config via `requireConfig()`, create `BrainHttpClient`
5. Validate entity via `client.getEntityDetail(entityArg)` — confirms it exists in workspace, gets display name
6. Generate CLAUDE.md content from template (see below)
7. Write between `<!-- brain-map-start -->` / `<!-- brain-map-end -->` markers (same idempotent pattern as `init.ts` lines 141-166)

**`runUnmap()`** — `brain unmap <dir>`

1. Read CLAUDE.md in target dir
2. Remove content between markers
3. Delete file if empty after removal

**Templates** — minimal, no stale data, agents resolve live context via MCP tools:

**Project:**
```markdown
# Brain: Project — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain project `{id}`.
- `get_project_context` with `project_id: "{id}"` for decisions, tasks, constraints
- `check_constraints` with `project: "{id}"` before architectural changes
- Scope `create_provisional_decision` / `ask_question` with `context: { project: "{id}" }`
```

**Feature** (with optional `--project`):
```markdown
# Brain: Feature — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain feature `{id}`.
[if --project: Parent project: `{project_id}` — use as `project_id` for project-scoped tools.]
[else: Resolve parent project via `get_entity_detail` with `entity_id: "feature:{id}"`.]
- Scope decisions/questions with `context: { feature: "{id}" }`
```

**Task** (with optional `--project`):
```markdown
# Brain: Task — "{name}"
This directory maps to brain task `{id}`.
- `get_task_context` with `task_id: "{id}"` — auto-resolves project and loads full subgraph
- `update_task_status` with `task_id: "{id}"` to track progress
- `create_subtask` with `parent_task_id: "{id}"` to decompose work
```

### 2. Modify: `cli/brain.ts`

Add two cases to the switch (dynamic imports, like `mcp`):

```typescript
case "map": {
  const { runMap } = await import("./commands/map");
  await runMap();
  break;
}
case "unmap": {
  const { runUnmap } = await import("./commands/map");
  await runUnmap();
  break;
}
```

Add to `printHelp()`:
```
  brain map <dir> <type:id>    Map directory to a brain entity (project, feature, task)
  brain unmap <dir>            Remove brain mapping from a directory
```

## Key decisions

- **No server changes**: `getEntityDetail` validates entity existence. Ancestry (feature→project) is resolved by agents at runtime via existing MCP tools, not baked into static CLAUDE.md.
- **Optional `--project <id>`**: Convenience flag for feature/task mappings. When provided, embeds project ID in template. When omitted, template instructs agent to discover it.
- **Templates in `map.ts`**, not `init-content.ts`: map is a distinct concern. Init-content exists because the compiled binary can't read plugin files. Map templates are just string constants local to the command.
- **Different markers** (`brain-map-start/end` vs `brain-plugin-start/end`): allows both init and map to coexist in the same CLAUDE.md.

## Files to modify

| File | Action |
|------|--------|
| `cli/commands/map.ts` | Create — all map/unmap logic and templates |
| `cli/brain.ts` | Edit — add `map`/`unmap` cases + help text |

## Reuse

- `cli/config.ts` → `requireConfig()` for auth
- `cli/http-client.ts` → `BrainHttpClient.getEntityDetail()` for validation
- `cli/commands/init.ts` lines 141-166 → marker-based CLAUDE.md write pattern

## Verification

1. `brain map ./some-dir project:real-id` → creates `./some-dir/CLAUDE.md` with project template
2. `brain map ./some-dir feature:real-id --project real-project-id` → creates feature template with embedded project
3. `brain map ./some-dir feature:real-id` → creates feature template without project (runtime resolution)
4. Re-run same command → idempotent update between markers
5. `brain unmap ./some-dir` → removes brain-map block
6. `brain map ./bad-dir project:nonexistent` → error: entity not found
7. Verify CLAUDE.md loads correctly by running Claude Code in the mapped directory

This directory maps to brain feature `{id}`.
-[if --project: Parent project: `{project_id}` — use as `project_id` for project-scoped tools.]

Done. Here's what was built:

**`cli/commands/map.ts`** (new) — Two commands:
- `brain map <dir> <type:id> [--project <id>]` — validates entity via server, writes CLAUDE.md with context-loading instructions between `<!-- brain-map-start/end -->` markers
- `brain unmap <dir>` — removes the mapping block, deletes file if empty

**`cli/brain.ts`** (modified) — Added `map`/`unmap` cases + help text

The generated CLAUDE.md templates include **"On first access"** instructions that tell agents to call `get_project_context` or `get_task_context` when they first touch files in the directory. This turns directory entry into automatic graph context loading, scoped to the right entity — without the graph knowing anything about the filesystem.

content: but shouldnt claude automatically do the initial mapping?

message.content: but shouldnt claude automatically do the initial mapping?

input.thought:
The user is asking: shouldn't Claude automatically do the initial mapping? 

They mean: when `brain init` runs on a repo, or when an agent starts a session, it should automatically scan the directory structure and map directories to brain graph entities — rather than requiring the user to manually run `brain map` for each directory.

How would this work? A few options:

1. **During `brain init`**: After setting up auth, fetch workspace projects, then ask the user to map directories to projects. Or auto-detect based on directory names matching project names.

2. **During session start (`brain system load-context`)**: The hook already loads workspace context with project names. It could scan the repo for directories that match project/feature names and suggest mappings.

3. **As part of the CLAUDE.md instructions**: Tell the agent "when you notice you're working in a directory that maps to a known project/feature, run `brain map` to persist the mapping."

4. **Auto-mapping in `brain init`**: Fetch projects from the workspace, show them to the user, let them pick which directory maps to which project. Then write CLAUDE.md files.

Option 4 is the most natural. During `brain init`, after auth succeeds, we already know the workspace projects (the init command could fetch them). We could add an interactive step: "Map directories to projects?" and let the user specify.

But actually, the simplest version: `brain init` already knows the workspace projects. It could add a step that lists projects and asks the user to map each one to a directory. But this is interactive — the CLI runs in hooks and non-interactive contexts too.

Actually wait — `brain init` IS interactive (the user runs it manually). So adding a mapping step there makes sense.

Or even simpler: the agent itself should do the mapping. When a coding agent starts working in a repo with brain init'd, and it loads workspace context (which includes project names), and it sees the directory structure, it could automatically run `brain map` for obvious matches. The CLAUDE.md instructions from `brain init` could tell the agent to do this.

Actually, I think the user might mean something even simpler: the `brain init` command should auto-detect and map directories based on the workspace's project/feature structure. Like:
- Fetch workspace projects
- For each project, try to match it to a top-level directory by name

export const BRAIN_CLAUDE_MD = `# Brain Knowledge Graph Integration

This project is connected to the Brain knowledge graph via MCP tools. The graph contains decisions, constraints, tasks, questions, and observations from all agents and humans working on this workspace.

## Project Context

At session start you receive a list of workspace projects with their IDs. Most MCP tools require a \`project_id\` — infer it from context:

- **Task-scoped work**: If you're given a task ID, call \`get_entity_detail\` — the task's graph relationships reveal its project.
- **Directory-scoped work**: Match the directory you're working in to a project. In a monorepo, each service/package directory typically maps to a project. In a monolith, a module or subpackage may map to a project or feature.
- **Single project**: If the workspace has only one project, use it.
- **Ambiguous**: If you can't determine the project, ask the user.

## Hooks

input.old_string:
## Project Context

At session start you receive a list of workspace projects with their IDs. Most MCP tools require a \`project_id\` — infer it from context:

- **Task-scoped work**: If you're given a task ID, call \`get_entity_detail\` — the task's graph relationships reveal its project.
- **Directory-scoped work**: Match the directory you're working in to a project. In a monorepo, each service/package directory typically maps to a project. In a monolith, a module or subpackage may map to a project or feature.
- **Single project**: If the workspace has only one project, use it.
- **Ambiguous**: If you can't determine the project, ask the user.
input.new_string:
## Project Context

At session start you receive a list of workspace projects with their IDs. Most MCP tools require a \`project_id\` — infer it from context:

- **Task-scoped work**: If you're given a task ID, call \`get_entity_detail\` — the task's graph relationships reveal its project.
- **Directory-scoped work**: Match the directory you're working in to a project. In a monorepo, each service/package directory typically maps to a project. In a monolith, a module or subpackage may map to a project or feature.
- **Single project**: If the workspace has only one project, use it.
- **Ambiguous**: If you can't determine the project, ask the user.

## Directory Mapping

Directories can be mapped to brain entities (project, feature, task) via \`CLAUDE.md\` files. When a directory has a brain mapping, any agent working in it automatically knows which entity to scope its work to.

**Auto-mapping**: When you determine which project or feature a directory corresponds to (from workspace context, task scope, or user input), check if the directory already has a brain mapping by looking for \`<!-- brain-map-start -->\` in its \`CLAUDE.md\`. If not, persist the mapping by running:

\`\`\`
brain map <directory> <type>:<id>
\`\`\`

Examples:
- \`brain map ./services/auth project:abc123\`
- \`brain map ./services/auth/oauth feature:def456 --project abc123\`
- \`brain map ./src/billing task:ghi789 --project abc123\`

This creates a \`CLAUDE.md\` in the target directory with context-loading instructions. Future agents entering that directory will automatically load the right graph context.

**When to map**: Map a directory when you confidently identify it as the primary location for a brain entity. Do not map speculatively — only when the project/feature/task clearly corresponds to the directory scope.
